title: Platform Channels
objectives: Understand platform channels via a real-life analogy
Send / receive typed messages between Dart and native (Kotlin / Swift)
Know when to use Pigeon for type-safety

sections.markdown:
**Think of an intercom system between the front office and the kitchen at a restaurant.**

- **Flutter** runs on ONE side (the front office, taking orders). **Native code** (Kotlin on Android, Swift on iOS, C++ on desktop) runs on the other side (the kitchen). They live in the same building (the app process) but on different floors.
- **A platform channel is the intercom.** You press a button, speak your message, the other side hears it and can respond.
- **`MethodChannel`** — 'I press the intercom and ASK for something: fry me an egg.' The other side responds when done: 'here's your egg.' Request/response.
- **`EventChannel`** — 'I open a live channel: keep me updated whenever a new dish is ready.' The kitchen streams updates until Flutter cancels.
- **`BasicMessageChannel`** — 'Just relay this note; no strict request/response semantics.'
- **`Pigeon`** — someone writes a template of every intercom conversation you can have, and generates the intercom on both sides so you never mis-say a message. Type-safe.

**Why this matters:** any time Flutter needs functionality that only the OS provides — battery level, biometrics, custom camera, background service, native SDK from a partner — platform channels are the bridge. Every serious Flutter app uses them, usually via a plugin.

**Platform channels** are Flutter's message-passing bridge between Dart code and platform-native code (Kotlin/Java on Android, Swift/Objective-C on iOS, C++ on desktop, JS on web). Messages are serialized via a `MessageCodec` (default: standard binary codec supporting nulls, bools, numbers, strings, byte arrays, lists, maps). Three flavors: **`MethodChannel`** (call methods with args, get a result), **`EventChannel`** (subscribe to a stream), **`BasicMessageChannel`** (raw two-way messaging). Modern Flutter apps use **Pigeon** for type-safe channel generation.
## The three channel types

rows: sensor readings, location updates, keyboard visibility
custom protocols, rare in app code

sections.markdown: ## Sample program — battery level (MethodChannel)
## EventChannel — a stream of sensor updates
## Pigeon — type-safe channels (recommended)
## Common beginner mistakes
**Mistake 1: Passing complex custom objects.** The default codec supports primitives + lists/maps of primitives. Pass a `Map<String, dynamic>` or use Pigeon which handles typed classes.

**Mistake 2: Calling channels from a background isolate.** Channels only work from the main isolate. Use `RootIsolateToken` + `BackgroundIsolateBinaryMessenger` in modern Flutter for background-isolate access.

**Mistake 3: Forgetting to handle errors on either side.** Dart: `try` around `invokeMethod`, catch `PlatformException`. Native: call `result.error(...)` on failure — otherwise the Dart Future hangs forever.
**Mistake 4: Not canceling EventChannel subscriptions.** Sensors keep running, drain battery, leak. `subscription.cancel()` in `dispose`.
**Mistake 5: Reinventing existing plugins.** Before writing a channel, search pub.dev — there's likely already a `battery_plus`, `geolocator`, `permission_handler`, etc.
## Best practices
**1. Prefer an existing plugin from pub.dev.** Save yourself weeks — well-maintained ones exist for battery, biometrics, WebView, permissions, camera, sensors.
**2. If you must write channels, use Pigeon.** Type-safe, generated on both sides, catches drift at compile time.
**3. Keep channels thin.** Put business logic in Dart; native side should be a shallow wrapper over OS APIs.
**4. Handle errors on both sides.** Send `result.error()` on native failure; catch `PlatformException` on Dart.
**5. Cancel EventChannel subscriptions in `dispose`.** Leaks drain battery.
**6. Version your channel names.** `com.myapp/battery-v2` if the contract changes; avoids silent breakage during migrations.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Battery level.** Implement the sample end-to-end (Android + iOS).
**Practice 2 (Medium) — EventChannel accelerometer.** Stream accelerometer data on both platforms; render a bar chart.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Convert to Pigeon.** Take the battery MethodChannel and regenerate with Pigeon; typed API on both sides.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Biometric prompt.** Bridge `BiometricPrompt` (Android) / `LAContext` (iOS) — use `local_auth` plugin as a reference for structure.
**Practice 5 (Expert) — Custom camera plugin.** Wrap `CameraX` / `AVFoundation`; expose a TextureView the Flutter side displays.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is a platform channel and why is it needed?**

**A:** A platform channel is Flutter's message-passing bridge between Dart code and platform-native code (Kotlin/Java on Android, Swift/Objective-C on iOS, C++ on desktop, JS on web). Needed because Dart doesn't have direct access to platform-specific APIs — you can't call `BatteryManager` or `Bluetooth` or `HealthKit` from Dart. The channel gives Dart a way to invoke native methods with arguments, receive results, and subscribe to native streams. Every non-trivial Flutter app uses platform channels — either directly, or (more commonly) through a plugin from pub.dev that wraps them.

**Q2: What are the three channel types?**

**A:** **(1) `MethodChannel`** — request/response. Dart calls `invokeMethod('foo', args)` and awaits a single result. Native side sets a method-call handler and calls `result.success(x)` or `result.error(...)`. Best for one-shot operations. **(2) `EventChannel`** — subscribe/stream. Dart calls `receiveBroadcastStream()`, gets a `Stream`. Native side implements a `StreamHandler` and calls `events.success(...)` multiple times. Best for continuous data (sensors, location). **(3) `BasicMessageChannel`** — raw two-way messages with a custom codec. Rare in app code; useful for custom protocols. **Rule:** MethodChannel for one-shots, EventChannel for streams, BasicMessageChannel almost never.

**Q4: What data types can travel through platform channels?**

**A:** The default codec (`StandardMessageCodec`) supports: `null`, `bool`, `int` (`int32` or `int64`), `double`, `String`, `Uint8List`, `Int32List`, `Int64List`, `Float64List`, and `List`/`Map` of any of these. **Cannot pass:** custom classes directly — you must serialize to a `Map<String, dynamic>` or use Pigeon (which generates class serialization). Byte arrays travel efficiently as `Uint8List`. For huge payloads, consider passing a file path or use a shared memory region instead.

**Q5: Can you call a platform channel from a background isolate?**

**A:** Historically NO — channels only worked on the main isolate because they needed the platform message dispatcher. **Modern Flutter (3.7+)** added `RootIsolateToken` + `BackgroundIsolateBinaryMessenger.ensureInitialized(token)` — pass the token from the main isolate, initialize the binary messenger on the background isolate, then channels work there too. Use case: a WorkManager callback isolate that needs to talk to native code. This is essential for reliable background work in modern Flutter apps.

**Q6: What is a Flutter plugin?**

**A:** A plugin is a Dart package that includes both a Dart-side API AND native code (Android/iOS/etc.) implementing platform channels. Consumers add it to `pubspec.yaml`; the plugin's Dart API delegates to its native code via channels. **Plugin structure:** `lib/` (Dart), `android/` (Kotlin), `ios/` (Swift), `macos/`/`linux/`/`windows/`/`web/` for each supported platform. **Federated plugins** split the Dart interface (`plugin`) from per-platform implementations (`plugin_android`, `plugin_ios`) so third parties can add platforms. Popular examples: `camera`, `geolocator`, `permission_handler`, `local_auth`, `webview_flutter`. Reach for a plugin BEFORE writing your own channel.

**Q7: How do you debug a platform channel?**

**A:** **Dart side:** wrap `invokeMethod` in try/catch, log `PlatformException.code` + `.message`. **Android:** attach `adb logcat | grep MyApp` to see native `Log.d()` output; use Android Studio's breakpoints in the Kotlin code (attach debugger to the running Flutter process). **iOS:** attach Xcode's debugger to the Runner target; add `print(...)` in Swift; use Xcode's console. **Verify channel name matches** on both sides (typo = silent hang). **Trace with `flutter run -v`** — verbose Flutter output shows channel activity. **`dart:developer` `log()`** shows in DevTools. The typical bug is name-typo or forgetting to call `result.success` — Dart waits forever.

## Real-world usage

- **`battery_plus`, `geolocator`, `sensors_plus`** — pure MethodChannel + EventChannel wrappers.
- **`camera`, `webview_flutter`** — plugins with a native `PlatformView` (embedding native views in Flutter).
- **`firebase_*`** — massive federated plugins with per-platform implementations.
- **`local_auth`** — biometric prompt bridge.
- **`permission_handler`** — runtime permission request bridge.
- **`workmanager`** — background execution with channel-based communication back to Dart.
- **Enterprise apps** — company-specific native SDKs (payment gateways, tracking libs) often wrapped in custom channels.

## Advanced notes

- **`PlatformView`** — embed a native UIView / Android View inside a Flutter widget (rare, expensive). Used by WebView.
- **`FlutterPlatformViewController`** — the machinery behind PlatformView.
- **`BinaryMessenger`** — the low-level transport; channels are wrappers.
- **`Method codecs`** — StandardMessageCodec (default), JSONMessageCodec, StringCodec, BinaryCodec.
- **Isolates + channels** — `RootIsolateToken` + `BackgroundIsolateBinaryMessenger.ensureInitialized(token)`.
- **Federated plugins** — `platform_interface` package pattern.
- **`FFI` (Foreign Function Interface)** — alternative for calling C libraries directly (see FFI topic); MUCH faster than channels for hot paths.
- **Debugging** — `--verbose` flutter run, `flutter logs`, `flutter attach`.
- **Testing** — mock channels in unit tests with `defaultBinaryMessenger.setMockMethodCallHandler`.

flashcards.back: Pigeon — generate typed API on both sides from a Dart interface declaration.
null/bool/int/double/String/typed lists/List/Map of these. No custom classes without Pigeon.
Check pub.dev — likely a plugin exists.
Channel name typo or forgetting result.success() → Dart Future hangs forever.

options: Serialize to Map or use Pigeon

challenges.text: Battery level MethodChannel end-to-end on Android.
Add iOS side; verify from both platforms.
EventChannel for accelerometer; render as a live bar chart.
Handle errors on both sides — `result.error(...)` and `PlatformException`.
Bridge a native biometric prompt (BiometricPrompt / LAContext).
Federated plugin: interface + Android impl + iOS impl in a single pub package.
